name: Nepal
tagline: From the Buddha's birthplace and the valley of art to a Himalayan federal republic.
summary: Nepal's recorded story runs from the early Kirata and Licchavi rulers of the Kathmandu Valley through the city-state kingdoms of the Mallas, whose artisans made the valley one of Asia's great centres of temple architecture. In its southern Tarai plain lies Lumbini, where the Buddha was born around the 6th century BCE. The modern state was forged by the Gorkha king Prithvi Narayan Shah, who conquered the valley in 1768–69 and unified dozens of hill principalities. Never formally colonised, Nepal endured a century of hereditary Rana rule before a series of democratic upheavals abolished the monarchy in 2008 and produced a federal democratic republic, ratified by a new constitution in 2015.

founding.label: Unification under the Shah dynasty
founding.detail: The modern Nepali state was founded when Prithvi Narayan Shah, king of the small Gorkha principality, conquered the three Malla kingdoms of the Kathmandu Valley — Kathmandu, Patan and Bhaktapur — in 1768–69 and made Kathmandu his capital, consolidating numerous hill principalities into a single kingdom. Nepal was never colonised; it abolished its monarchy in 2008 and became a federal democratic republic.

quickFacts.label: Unification
quickFacts.value: Kathmandu Valley conquered 1768–69 by Prithvi Narayan Shah

quickFacts.label: Colonisation
quickFacts.value: Never colonised; independence retained after 1814–16 war

quickFacts.label: Monarchy abolished
quickFacts.value: Declared a federal democratic republic, 28 May 2008

eras.title: Ancient Kingdoms: Kirata and Licchavi
eras.standfirst: In the fertile Kathmandu Valley, early Kirata rulers gave way to the Licchavi dynasty, whose Sanskrit inscriptions and Indian-style coins made the valley a cultural bridge between India and Tibet.
body: Nepali tradition recalls a line of early Kirata rulers in the Kathmandu Valley before the historical record becomes firm. A coherent dynastic history becomes possible, though with large gaps, only with the rise of the Licchavi dynasty around the 4th or 5th century CE. Probably of north Indian origin, the Licchavis used Sanskrit as a court language and issued Indian-style coins, while building economic and political relations both southward with India and northward with Tibet.

The Licchavi period — conventionally dated to roughly 450–750 CE — is the first era of Nepalese history documented by abundant evidence. Stone inscriptions, particularly full between about 500 and 700 CE, supplement the dynastic chronicles. Most Licchavi kings were devout Hindus, yet they did not impose Brahmanic social codes on their non-Hindu subjects, and Buddhism flourished alongside Hindu cults in the valley's temples and monasteries.

Sitting astride the trade routes that linked the Gangetic plain to the Tibetan plateau, the valley grew wealthy and cosmopolitan. The temple complex of Changu Narayan preserves one of the valley's earliest inscriptions, from the 5th century CE, and the Buddhist stupa of Swayambhu is reckoned the oldest such monument in the valley. By tradition the town of Kathmandu itself was founded in 723 CE, during the Licchavi age, by Raja Gunakamadeva.

events.title: Rise of the Licchavi dynasty
events.summary: The Licchavis establish the first well-documented dynasty of the Kathmandu Valley, using Sanskrit, minting coins and linking India and Tibet by trade.

events.title: Licchavi stone inscriptions
events.summary: Dense Licchavi-era inscriptions and chronicles make the valley one of the best-documented early states of the Himalayas, recording Hindu and Buddhist patronage alike.

events.title: Traditional founding of Kathmandu
events.summary: Tradition credits Raja Gunakamadeva with founding the town of Kathmandu during the Licchavi period; it would later give its name to the valley and the nation's capital.

eras.title: Lumbini and the Birth of the Buddha
eras.standfirst: In the Tarai plains of the south, the Shakya prince Siddhartha Gautama was born at Lumbini — a site whose authenticity the emperor Ashoka marked with a pillar two centuries later.
body: South of the Himalayan hills, in the flat Tarai plains that today form Nepal's border with India, lies Lumbini, revered as the birthplace of the Buddha. According to tradition the Shakya prince Siddhartha Gautama was born there to Queen Mayadevi, wife of King Suddhodana of Kapilavastu, while she journeyed between Tilaurakot and her family home at Devadaha. The conventional dating places his birth in the 6th century BCE, with the UNESCO inscription citing 623 BCE.

Siddhartha renounced princely life, attained enlightenment, and as the Buddha taught a path beyond suffering that would spread across Asia to become one of the world's great religions. Lumbini's standing as the authentic birthplace rests on unusually firm ground: the Mauryan emperor Ashoka visited about 249 BCE and erected a commemorative pillar bearing a Brahmi inscription, the earliest physical testimony to the site.

Archaeology at Lumbini has revealed Buddhist shrines, monasteries and stupas spanning from the 3rd century BCE onward, including brick structures beneath the Maya Devi Temple and the marker stone said to fix the spot of the birth. Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1997, Lumbini remains one of the holiest pilgrimage centres of Buddhism and anchors Nepal's claim to a place at the root of a world faith.

events.title: Birth of the Buddha at Lumbini
events.summary: Siddhartha Gautama, of the Shakya clan, is born at Lumbini to Queen Mayadevi and King Suddhodana of Kapilavastu; he later founds Buddhism.

events.title: Ashoka's pillar at Lumbini
events.summary: The Mauryan emperor Ashoka visits Lumbini and erects an inscribed sandstone pillar, the earliest material evidence identifying the Buddha's birthplace.

events.title: Lumbini inscribed as World Heritage
events.summary: UNESCO inscribes Lumbini on the World Heritage List, recognising it as the birthplace of the Buddha and a centre of Buddhist pilgrimage since the 3rd century BCE.

figures.name: Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha)
figures.role: Founder of Buddhism, born at Lumbini
figures.blurb: A prince of the Shakya clan born at Lumbini in the Nepali Tarai. After renouncing his royal life and attaining enlightenment, he taught a path beyond suffering whose followers spread Buddhism across Asia. His birthplace gives Nepal a foundational place in the history of a world religion.

pullquote.text: The Lord Buddha was born in the sacred area of Lumbini, in the Tarai plains of southern Nepal.

eras.title: The Malla City-States and the Valley of Art
eras.standfirst: Under the Malla kings the Kathmandu Valley fractured into the rival city-states of Kathmandu, Patan and Bhaktapur, whose patronage produced some of the finest temple architecture in Asia.
body: From around the 13th century the Kathmandu Valley was ruled by the Malla dynasty, which introduced legal and social codes shaped by Hindu practice and became lavish patrons of art and architecture. The Mallas presided over a golden age of Newar craftsmanship: the tiered brick-and-timber temples, gilded shrines and intricately carved windows that still distinguish the valley's towns took their classic form under Malla rule.

The unity of the valley did not last. The ruler Yaksha Malla (reigned c. 1429–c. 1482) divided his kingdom among his sons, creating the independent principalities of Kathmandu, Patan and Bhaktapur (Bhadgaon). Each controlled territory in the surrounding hills and competed for the lucrative trade between Tibet and India that flowed through the valley, building rival palace complexes — the Durbar squares — at the heart of each city.

The three royal capitals became showcases of competitive splendour, their squares crowded with palaces, pagoda temples and public fountains. Today the Durbar squares of Kathmandu (Hanuman Dhoka), Patan and Bhaktapur, together with the stupas of Swayambhu and Bauddhanath and the temples of Pashupati and Changu Narayan, form the seven monument zones of the Kathmandu Valley UNESCO World Heritage Site. By the early 18th century, weakened by family feuds and economic discontent, the Malla city-states fell prey to a rising hill power to the west.

events.title: Malla dynasty rules the valley
events.summary: The Mallas govern the Kathmandu Valley, codifying Hindu-influenced law and patronising the Newar temple architecture for which the valley becomes famous.

events.title: Valley divided into three kingdoms
events.summary: Yaksha Malla partitions the valley among his sons, founding the rival city-states of Kathmandu, Patan and Bhaktapur, which compete over Tibet–India trade.

events.title: The Durbar squares and Newar art
events.summary: Malla patronage produces the palace squares, pagoda temples and stupas later recognised as the seven monument zones of the Kathmandu Valley World Heritage Site.

figures.name: Yaksha Malla
figures.role: Malla king of the Kathmandu Valley
figures.blurb: The last ruler of a united valley, Yaksha Malla divided his realm among his sons, creating the independent principalities of Kathmandu, Patan and Bhaktapur. Their rivalry shaped the valley's competitive flowering of temple art and architecture until the Gorkha conquest.

eras.title: Gorkha Unification and the British War
eras.standfirst: Prithvi Narayan Shah's Gorkha armies welded the hill principalities into a single kingdom; expansion then collided with British India, ending in the Treaty of Sugauli and Nepal's enduring independence.
body: Prithvi Narayan Shah became king of the small hill state of Gorkha in 1742 and set out to absorb the quarrelsome principalities around it. His ethnically diverse army — soon famed as the Gurkhas — pressed toward the rich Kathmandu Valley. An early assault was repulsed after the raja of Kathmandu enlisted East India Company forces in 1767, but once those troops withdrew, Prithvi Narayan took Kathmandu in 1768, completing his conquest of the three Malla kingdoms by 1769 and founding the modern state of Nepal with its capital at Kathmandu.

His successors continued to expand the kingdom across the Himalayan foothills, pushing west toward the Sutlej and into territories that brought Nepal into conflict with the expanding British East India Company. A sharpening border dispute after 1801 ignited the Anglo-Nepalese (Gurkha) War of 1814–16, in which Nepali commanders won British respect for their tenacity even as the Company's superior resources told.

The war ended with the Treaty of Sugauli, signed on 4 March 1816. Nepal renounced its claims to the disputed Tarai lowlands and ceded its conquests in the west, but it remained independent: the British accepted a resident in Kathmandu with the status of an ambassador to a sovereign state rather than a controlling agent. Nepal thus avoided colonisation, retaining its monarchy and a guarded autonomy that would shape the next century.

events.title: Conquest of the Kathmandu Valley
events.summary: Prithvi Narayan Shah of Gorkha captures Kathmandu in 1768 and the Malla kingdoms by 1769, unifying the hill states into the kingdom of Nepal.

events.title: Anglo-Nepalese War
events.summary: A border dispute with British India erupts into the Gurkha War; Nepali resistance is fierce but the East India Company prevails.

events.title: Treaty of Sugauli
events.summary: Nepal cedes the Tarai and western conquests and admits a British resident, but keeps its independence and monarchy — never becoming a colony.

figures.name: Prithvi Narayan Shah
figures.role: Founder and first king of unified Nepal
figures.blurb: King of Gorkha from 1742, he conquered the three Malla kingdoms of the Kathmandu Valley in 1768–69 and consolidated dozens of petty principalities into the state of Nepal, establishing the capital at Kathmandu. His Gurkha soldiers became legendary, and his Shah dynasty ruled Nepal for nearly 240 years.

pullquote.text: Prithvi Narayan Shah conquered the three Malla kingdoms of Kathmandu, Patan and Bhadgaon in 1769 and consolidated them to found the modern state of Nepal.

eras.title: The Rana Century and the Return of the Crown
eras.standfirst: For a century hereditary Rana prime ministers ran Nepal in seclusion, until a 1950–51 revolution restored the Shah kings — who then built the autocratic panchayat order overthrown by the democracy movement of 1990.
body: In 1846 Jung Bahadur Rana seized power amid palace bloodshed, was named prime minister for life and granted the hereditary title of Rana. He and his descendants reduced the Shah kings to figureheads and ruled Nepal as hereditary prime ministers from 1846 until 1951, keeping the kingdom isolated and largely closed to the outside world while preserving its formal independence.

The Rana monopoly ended in a revolution. In 1950 King Tribhuvan fled the Rana grip, and a popular movement allied with him forced the restoration of crown authority in 1951, returning sovereignty to the Shah dynasty. Brief experiments with parliamentary democracy followed, but in 1960 King Mahendra dissolved parliament and banned political parties. The 1962 constitution entrenched the partyless panchayat system, a multitiered structure of councils that gave the king near-autocratic control.

Pressure for democracy built over the following decades. In 1990 nationwide unrest — the Jana Andolan, or People's Movement — forced King Birendra to lift the ban on political parties and accept a multiparty constitutional monarchy. The panchayat era was over, but the new democratic order would soon face a violent challenge from a Maoist insurgency rooted in the countryside's deep inequalities.

events.title: Jung Bahadur founds Rana rule
events.summary: After a violent seizure of power, Jung Bahadur becomes prime minister for life with the hereditary title of Rana, sidelining the Shah kings for a century.

events.title: Revolution restores the monarchy
events.summary: A palace revolt and popular movement end Rana dominance and restore the sovereignty of the Shah crown under King Tribhuvan.

events.title: Panchayat autocracy
events.summary: King Mahendra dissolves parliament in 1960 and bans parties; the 1962 constitution installs the partyless panchayat system under royal control.

events.title: People's Movement restores democracy
events.summary: Nationwide protests force King Birendra to legalise political parties and accept a multiparty parliamentary system, ending the panchayat era.

figures.name: Jung Bahadur Rana
figures.role: Prime minister and founder of the Rana dynasty
figures.blurb: He gained control of the government in 1846, was named prime minister for life and given the hereditary title Rana. The Ranas ruled Nepal as hereditary prime ministers, eclipsing the Shah kings, until the office was overthrown in 1951.

eras.title: Insurgency, Republic and Reconstruction
eras.standfirst: A decade-long Maoist war ended the monarchy and gave Nepal a federal democratic republic in 2008; a devastating 2015 earthquake and a new constitution defined the years that followed.
body: In 1996 the Communist Party of Nepal (Maoist) launched an armed insurgency that spread through the countryside and, over the following decade, claimed more than 12,000 lives. The conflict intensified after the royal massacre of 2001 and a royal seizure of power, but in 2006 a UN-mediated peace accord brought the Maoists into mainstream politics. In December 2007 the parties agreed the monarchy would be abolished, and on 28 May 2008 a newly elected assembly voted to end more than two centuries of royal rule and declare Nepal a democratic republic.

On 25 April 2015 a moment-magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck central Nepal near Gorkha, followed by a magnitude-7.3 aftershock seventeen days later. About 9,000 people were killed and hundreds of thousands of structures were damaged or destroyed; the Durbar squares of the Kathmandu Valley and Kathmandu's landmark Dharahara tower were among the heritage casualties. It was Nepal's deadliest disaster since the 1934 earthquake, and reconstruction of homes and monuments stretched on for years afterward.

Months later, on 20 September 2015, Nepal promulgated a permanent constitution establishing a secular federal republic of seven provinces with a bicameral Federal Parliament. The years that followed brought unstable coalition governments and repeated changes of prime minister. In September 2025 the so-called Gen Z protests toppled the government of K.P. Sharma Oli; an interim administration organised fresh elections, and by 2026 a new government had taken office under the young reformist figure Balendra Shah, the republic's institutions enduring through the turbulence.

events.title: Maoist insurgency
events.summary: The Communist Party of Nepal (Maoist) wages a decade-long armed insurgency that kills more than 12,000 people before a 2006 UN-mediated peace accord.

events.title: Monarchy abolished, republic declared
events.summary: A newly elected constituent assembly ends more than two centuries of Shah rule and declares Nepal a federal democratic republic.

events.title: Gorkha earthquake
events.summary: A magnitude-7.8 quake near Gorkha kills about 9,000 people and devastates Kathmandu Valley heritage, including Durbar squares and the Dharahara tower.

events.title: Federal constitution promulgated
events.summary: Nepal adopts a permanent constitution establishing a secular federal republic of seven provinces with a bicameral Federal Parliament.

figures.name: Pushpa Kamal Dahal 'Prachanda'
figures.blurb: Leader of the Communist Party of Nepal (Maoist) during the 1996–2006 insurgency, he later entered mainstream politics and served several terms as prime minister of the republic, including a term beginning in December 2022.

pullquote.text: On May 28, 2008, more than two centuries of royal rule came to an end as the new assembly voted to declare Nepal a democratic republic.

figures.name: Prithvi Narayan Shah
figures.blurb: King of Gorkha who conquered the Kathmandu Valley in 1768–69 and united the hill principalities into the modern kingdom of Nepal, founding the Shah dynasty.

figures.name: Jung Bahadur Rana
figures.blurb: Seized power in 1846 and became hereditary prime minister, establishing the Rana dynasty that ruled Nepal in the kings' shadow until 1951.

figures.name: B. P. Koirala
figures.role: Democratic leader and first elected prime minister
figures.blurb: A founder of the Nepali Congress and a central figure in the struggle against Rana rule, Bishweshwar Prasad Koirala became Nepal's first democratically elected prime minister in 1959 before the king's 1960 takeover.

sources.label: Lumbini, the Birthplace of the Lord Buddha
